summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while developers should first strive to write self-explanatory code, comments remain essential for explaining the 'why' behind implementation decisions. He emphasizes that code should be written primarily for human readers, not machines, citing classics like SICP and Knuth's literate programming. Through a concrete example, he demonstrates how poor code cluttered with inline comments can be refactored into cleaner, self-documenting code using meaningful names. However, he ultimately concludes that comments cannot be fully replaced because code alone cannot explain rationale, rejected alternatives, or contextual decisions.
key_points: Write code for human readers first, compilers second - this naturally reduces the need for excessive comments
Bad comments often serve as a crutch for poorly written code; refactoring with meaningful names is preferable
Comments are irreplaceable for explaining why decisions were made, not just how code works
More comments doesn't mean more readable code - sometimes fewer comments with better naming improves clarity
What seems obvious to one developer may be completely opaque to another without context
money_quotes: Programs must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.
The best kind of comments are the ones you don't need.
Code can't explain why the program is being written, and the rationale for choosing this or that method.
The comments in the snippet above – if you even noticed them – only clutter the code even more.
Code can only tell you how the program works; comments can tell you why it works.
Let us concentrate rather on explaining to human beings what we want a computer to do.
What is perfectly, transparently obvious to one developer may be utterly opaque to another developer who has no context.
key_insight: Self-documenting code and good comments serve complementary purposes: code explains the how, while comments explain the why, and neither can fully substitute for the other.
